elements.sent-2411-065: The algorithm we use here to search for H{\sc i signatures consists of a spectral stacking routine combined with a one-dimensional spectral-line finder.}
elements.sent-2411-066: These two functions are wrapped into a publicly-available Python package\footnote{https://github.com/SJVeronese/nicci-package.. .}
elements.sent-2411-067: Throughout the paper we refer to the spectral stacking task as STACKER and to the line finding task as FINDER.
elements.sent-2411-068: In the following sections we describe each task in detail and we refer to Sect. \ref{sec:calibration for details on how the values of the input parameters were determined.}
elements.sent-2411-069: STACKER begins with several preliminary manipulations on the input data cube:.
elements.sent-2411-070: For this we used the SoFiA-2 detection mask that was also used to create the moment maps as described in \citet{mhongoose2 ;.}
elements.sent-2411-071: It consists of shifting each spaxel along the spectral axis so that its central channel corresponds to the value of the same LoS in the velocity field.
elements.sent-2411-072: To preserve the noise properties of the data, the shifting wraps the spectrum around\footnote{The spectral alignment is performed using our implementation of the GIPSY \citep{gipsy,gipsy2 task SHUFFLE.}}
elements.sent-2411-073: For the TNG50 galaxies we initially used the noiseless moment 1 map, as it contains the kinematical information of the gas outside the SoFiA-2 detection mask (see Sect. \ref{sec:testing ).}
elements.sent-2411-074: As this information is missing when dealing with real data, we tested several velocity field assumptions as described in Sect. \ref{sec:assumption ;.}
elements.sent-2411-075: To achieve this, the pixel size is increased to match the beam size\footnote{The regridding is done via the reproject\_interp function from the Astropy Reproject package \citep{astropy13,astropy18,astropy22 .}}
elements.sent-2411-076: The interpolation is done using a nearest-neighbour method to preserve the underlying noise properties.
elements.sent-2411-077: Using different interpolation orders or Reproject functions leads to negligible improvement, but a significant cost in terms of computing time.. .
elements.sent-2411-078: We do not apply any primary beam correction at this stage because stacking requires the noise to be as uniform as possible.
elements.sent-2411-079: A primary beam correction increases the $\sigma$ level toward the outskirts of the FoV.
elements.sent-2411-080: The stacked spectrum for each region is then computed as follow: \begin{equation}\label{eq:stack} F_S=\frac{\sum_iF_i\cdot w_i}{\sum_i w_i} ,\end{equation} where $F_S$ is the stacked flux in a given channel, calculated by summing the fluxes ($F_i$) of the spectra in that channel, weighted by their respective weights ($w_i$).
elements.sent-2411-081: The common weighting scheme used in the literature is the so-called `$\sigma^2$-weight' (e.g., \citealt{fabello11,kanekar16,ianjam18,hiss,healy21c,chowdhury22,apurba23 ): $w_i=\frac{1 {\sigma_i^2}$, where $\sigma_i$ represents the $i^{th}$-spectrum's noise level.}}
elements.sent-2411-082: As the presence of signal hidden in the noise potentially increases $\sigma_i$, leading to the down weighting of the corresponding spectrum and thus reducing the contribution of any hidden signal, we also considered the `equal-weight' scheme ($w_i=1$ $\forall$ $i$) and tested against the $\sigma^2$-weight as described in Sect. \ref{sec:calibration .}

elements.sent-2411-083: FINDER employs a simplified one-dimensional version of the SOFIA-2 smooth-and-clip (S$+$C) algorithm, a procedure involving smoothing the data on multiple scales and apply for each scale a $\sigma$-clip \citep{serra12b .}
elements.sent-2411-084: The output is binary masks, one for each stacked spectrum, which are subsequently used to create the source catalogue by combining the detected channels in each mask, i.e., assigning consecutive detections to a single source.
elements.sent-2411-085: Positive and negative sources spanning fewer than a given number of channels are removed from the catalogue.
elements.sent-2411-086: Each source is placed in a three-dimensional space ($\frac{F_{max {\sigma}$, $\frac{F_{sum {\sigma}$ and $\frac{F_{mean {\sigma}$), where $F_{max}$, $F_{sum}$ and $F_{mean}$ are the peak, total and mean flux (see Fig. \ref{fig:reliability for an example of the parameter space plot).}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-087: For each positive source $P_i=(F_{i,max ,F_{i,sum ,F_{i,mean )$ a volume density of positive ($n_{pos}$) and negative ($n_{neg}$) sources, as determined on the collection of stacked spectra, is calculated through the Gaussian kernel density estimation \citep{hastie01 : \begin{equation}n=\frac{1}{h}\sum_i\mathcal{G}\left(\frac{x-x_i}{h}\right) ,\end{equation} where $x=(x_1,x_2,...,x_n)$ are the position of the sources, $\mathcal{G}$ is a Gaussian kernel and $h$ is a smoothing factor.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-088: The reliability ($\varrho$) of $P_i$ is then given by \begin{equation}\varrho=\begin{cases} \frac{n_{pos}-n_{neg}}{n_{pos}}, \text{for }n_{pos}\geq n_{neg} 0, \text{otherwise} \end{cases} .\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-089: Only sources for which $\varrho$ is above a given threshold are retained in the final source catalogue.
elements.sent-2411-090: MeerKAT noise is known to be Gaussian to a high degree, as illustrated in Sect. \ref{sec:noise .}
elements.sent-2411-091: To quantify this further we incorporated two Gaussianity tests in FINDER.
elements.sent-2411-092: The first is the Anderson-Darling test \citep{stephens74,stephens76,stephens77,stephens79 , which evaluates the hypothesis that a given sample follows a specific distribution, in our case, a Gaussian distribution.}
elements.sent-2411-093: This statistical test quantifies the disparity between the observed data and the expected values by computing the Anderson-Darling statistic $A$ \begin{equation}A^2=-n-\sum _{i=1}^{n}{\frac {2i-1}{n}}[\ln(F(x_{i}))+\ln(1-F(x_{n+1-i}))] ,\end{equation} where $n$ is the number of elements in the sample, $F$ is the hypothesized cumulative distribution function (in our case, a Gaussian) and $x_i$ is the $i^{th}$-datapoint in progressive order ($x_1<\cdots<x_{n}$).
elements.sent-2411-094: Conversely, a higher statistic indicates a poorer fit, meaning non-Gaussian behaviour.
elements.sent-2411-095: A p-value \begin{equation}p=\begin{cases} e^{1.2937-5.709A+0.0186A^2}, \text{if }A\geq0.6 e^{0.9177-4.279A-1.38A^2}, \text{if }0.34\leq A<0.6 e^{-8.318+42.796A-59.938A^2}, \text{if }0.2\leq A<0.34 e^{13.436+101.14A-223.73A^2}, \text{otherwise} \end{cases} ,\end{equation} is associated with the outcome of the test.
elements.sent-2411-096: Since $p=0.05$ is a `standard' value for rejecting the alternative hypothesis, in our case spectra for which $p<0.05$ are considered to have non-Gaussian noise.
elements.sent-2411-097: The $\sigma$ before and after the smoothing is then compared, with the expectation that it should decrease by a factor of $\sqrt{10}$ if the noise adheres to a Gaussian distribution.

elements.sent-2411-229: One of the preliminary steps of our stacking procedure is to regrid the cube such that the pixel size is upscaled to match the beam size.
elements.sent-2411-230: One potential effect of the regridding is to lose flux and thus detections.
elements.sent-2411-231: The argument is that after regridding the cube violates the Nyquist sampling criterion, as now the pixel size does not match the longest baseline of the interferometer, impling information is lost.
elements.sent-2411-232: To check how important this may be for our stacking experiment, we apply the procedure presented in Sect. \ref{sec:calibration without regridding.}
elements.sent-2411-233: The comparison between the two figures is showing that without regridding we pick up less sources.
elements.sent-2411-234: We think that the reason for this counter-intuitive conclusion is that the features we aim to pick up via stacking, i.e., H{\sc i clouds, are sufficiently large that even after regridding they span more than 2.2 beams, satisfying so the Nyquist sampling.}
elements.figure-2411-019: \includegraphics[width=\hsize]{calibration_overlay_noregrid.pdf} \caption{Stacking detection map without regridding the cube. The background grey-scale image is the cube of the TNG50 520855 galaxy collapsed along the spectral axis and blanked from the galaxy emission. Black contours denote the noiseless moment 0 map clipped at the column density value of $3.6\times10^{17}$ cm$^{-2}$. The coloured squares indicate the stacking regions where their stacked spectrum contains a reliable detection. Different colours represent different cell size: red for $9\times9$ and green for $5\times5$ beams.} \label{fig:calnoregrid}
elements.section-2411-010: Stacking is not equivalent to smoothing
elements.sent-2411-235: One can argue that the stacking procedure presented in this paper is equivalent to spectrally and spatially smoothing the data at different scales.
elements.sent-2411-236: To check whether this is true we run SoFiA-2 on the masked TNG50 520855 galaxy cube using a spatial smoothing kernel equal to our smallest cell size (i.e., $25\times25$ pixels, equivalent to $5\times5$ beams) and spectral smoothing kernels equal to what we have employed with our FINDER (i.e., 1, 7 and 12 channels.
elements.sent-2411-237: The reliability parameters (minimum SNR of 5 and reliability threshold of 0.85) and the minimum allowed spatial and spectral sizes for a source ($1\times1$ beam and 7 channels) were set to be equivalent to what we used in our stacking experiment.
elements.sent-2411-238: Comparing it with Fig. \ref{fig:cal , one can see that our stacking procedure is not equivalent to smoothing the cube at different scale.}
elements.sent-2411-239: Furthermore, given that the SoFiA-2 detections are outside the $3.6\times10^{17}$ cm$^{-2}$ column density level, we suspect that what was detected by SoFiA-2 is mainly noise.
elements.sent-2411-240: We want to stress that this comparison is meant to show the differences between our stacking and smoothing and not to compare our procedure with SoFiA-2.
elements.figure-2411-020: \includegraphics[width=\hsize]{calibration_overlay_sofia.pdf} \caption{Moment 0 map, blanked from the galaxy emission in yellow, produced by SoFiA-2 when emulating our stacking procedure via smoothing. Black contours denote the noiseless moment 0 map clipped at the column density value of $3.6\times10^{17}$ cm$^{-2}$.} \label{fig:smoothcal}
